name: expo-api-routes
description: Guidelines for creating API routes in Expo Router with EAS Hosting
capabilities: Create API endpoints using Expo Router file-based routing with +api.ts suffix
Implement HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) in server-side handlers
Handle query parameters, headers, and JSON request bodies
Access environment variables securely for API keys and database credentials
Configure CORS headers for cross-origin requests from web clients
Deploy API routes to EAS Hosting on Cloudflare Workers
author: expo
securitySummary: Educational documentation for Expo Router API routes. All static findings are false positives. Detected patterns (code blocks, API examples, environment variables) represent safe documentation demonstrating best practices for server-side API development.
valueStatement: Building server-side endpoints in Expo apps requires understanding file-based routing, secure credential handling, and deployment to cloud infrastructure. This skill provides guidelines for creating, testing, and deploying API routes using Expo Router and EAS Hosting.
userTitle: Create API Routes in Expo Router
limitations: No Node.js filesystem access on EAS Hosting (runs on Cloudflare Workers)
No native Node.js modules available (must use Web APIs or polyfills)
Cannot use WebSockets without Durable Objects configuration

useCases.targetUser: Mobile developers building full-stack Expo applications
useCases.title: Mobile App Backend API
useCases.description: Build server-side endpoints that handle database operations, authentication, and third-party API integrations for React Native mobile apps without exposing secrets to clients.

useCases.title: API Proxy for External Services
useCases.description: Create server-side routes that proxy requests to external APIs (OpenAI, Stripe, weather services) while keeping API keys secure on the server.

useCases.targetUser: Developers implementing payment or event-driven workflows
useCases.title: Webhook Handlers
useCases.description: Receive and process callbacks from services like Stripe payments, GitHub events, or other webhooks in a server-side Expo endpoint.

promptTemplates.title: Basic API Route
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a new API route at /api/hello that returns a JSON response with a greeting message.

promptTemplates.title: API Route with Authentication
promptTemplates.prompt: Create an API route at /api/users that accepts a POST request with user data, validates the input, and returns the created user with a 201 status code.
promptTemplates.scenario: Building a CRUD endpoint with validation

promptTemplates.title: API Route with External API
promptTemplates.prompt: Create an API route that calls the OpenAI API to generate a response. Use an environment variable for the API key and handle errors gracefully.

promptTemplates.title: Protected API Route
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a protected API route that requires a Bearer token in the Authorization header. Return 401 if missing or invalid.

bestPractices: Never expose API keys or secrets in client code - always use server-side environment variables
Validate and sanitize all user input before processing or storing data
Use appropriate HTTP status codes (200, 201, 400, 401, 404, 500) for different error scenarios
antiPatterns: Storing API keys or secrets in client-side code or environment files that ship to users
Skipping input validation and trusting user-provided data without sanitization
Returning detailed error messages that expose internal implementation details to clients

faq.question: What is the difference between API routes and regular Expo pages?
faq.answer: API routes export HTTP handler functions (GET, POST, etc.) and return raw Response objects. Regular pages export React components that render UI. API routes run server-side while pages render client-side.

faq.question: Can I use a database with API routes on EAS Hosting?
faq.answer: Yes. Since filesystem is unavailable on Cloudflare Workers, use cloud databases like Cloudflare D1, Turso, PlanetScale, Supabase, or Neon. These provide HTTP APIs that work in the serverless environment.

faq.question: How do I test API routes locally?
faq.answer: Run 'npx expo serve' to start the development server. API routes are available at http://localhost:8081/api/your-route. Use curl or Postman to test endpoints during development.

faq.question: What environment variables should I use for production?
faq.answer: Use eas env:create --name VAR_NAME --value value --environment production or configure variables in the Expo dashboard. Never commit .env files to version control.

faq.question: How long can API route handlers run on EAS Hosting?
faq.answer: EAS Hosting on Cloudflare Workers has a 30-second timeout for CPU-intensive tasks. Design your endpoints to complete quickly or use async processing for longer operations.

faq.question: Can API routes access the filesystem?
faq.answer: No. API routes run on Cloudflare Workers, which do not have Node.js filesystem access. Use cloud databases or object storage (S3, Cloudflare R2) for file persistence.
